coredata.dc:title: Carbon nanodot arrays grown as replicas of specially widened anodic aluminum oxide pore arrays
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2008 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, P.J.
Chen, J.T.
Zhuo, R.F.
Xu, L.
Lu, Q.H.
Ji, X.
Yan, P.X.
Wu, Z.G.
coredata.dc:description: Via a specially widened anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) pore arrays, carbon nanodot arrays with uniform size and high density were obtained through filtered cathodic arc plasma (FCAP) technique. The AAO template was prepared in oxalic acid by multi-steps to get a specially enlarged opening which plays an important role in the deposition of nanodots. The morphology of the nanodots was studied by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The diameter of the as-prepared nanodot demonstrated here is about 100nm at the bottom and less than 40nm at the top, and the density was estimated to 1010 cm−2. Field emission properties of the nanodot arrays were investigated and a low threshold field of 5.1V/μm at 10mA/cm2 was obtained. In this paper, the carbon nanodot arrays grown as replicas of the specially widened AAO template may support a strategy to realize the fabrication of nanodot arrays with various materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanodot arrays
Anodic aluminum oxide
Filtered cathodic arc plasma
Field emission

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the deposition of ions on typical AAO template of straight pores (a) and special AAO template with the pore openings enlarged (b) under the influence of the negative charges.

Schematic diagram of the fabrication processes of special AAO template. (a) Aluminum, (b) narrow pores were formed by anodization, (c) pores were widened by chemical etching, (d) AAO template with enlarged pore openings.

Cross-section images of sample A by two-step anodization (a), sample B (b) and sample C (c) with enlarged pore openings of different depth by multi-steps anodization. Surface views of sample A (d), sample B (e) and sample C (f).
Cross-section views of sample A (a), sample B (b) and sample C (c) after carbon deposition.
Surface view of the nanodot arrays after etched the AAO template. The inset shows the cross-section view of some nanodots with small pits on the tips.

Raman spectrum of the carbon films deposited on AAO template. Curve fitting results of two graphitic peaks D and G centered at 1322 and 1600cm−1, and T peak at 1029cm−1 associated with sp3 vibrations. The fitted peaks are correspondingly labeled.
Current density vs. electric field (J–E) curve of the carbon nanodot arrays with the inset being the Fowler–Nordheim plot.

head.ce:title: Carbon nanodot arrays grown as replicas of specially widened anodic aluminum oxide pore arrays

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Plasma & Metal Materials, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of New Nonferrous Metal Materials, Gansu University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, People's Republic of China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Physics Science and Technology, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, People's Republic of China. Tel.: +86 931 8912719; fax: +86 931 8913554.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Via a specially widened anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) pore arrays, carbon nanodot arrays with uniform size and high density were obtained through filtered cathodic arc plasma (FCAP) technique. The AAO template was prepared in oxalic acid by multi-steps to get a specially enlarged opening which plays an important role in the deposition of nanodots. The morphology of the nanodots was studied by a field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM). The diameter of the as-prepared nanodot demonstrated here is about 100nm at the bottom and less than 40nm at the top, and the density was estimated to 1010 cm−2. Field emission properties of the nanodot arrays were investigated and a low threshold field of 5.1V/μm at 10mA/cm2 was obtained. In this paper, the carbon nanodot arrays grown as replicas of the specially widened AAO template may support a strategy to realize the fabrication of nanodot arrays with various materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanostructures have attracted great interesting due to their unique physicochemical properties in the field of nano-device such as field emitter, data storage, sensor and other scientific interests [1–3]. Formation of well-ordered nanostructures with high density and uniform size is particularly important for the applications in nanodevices and still a great challenge. Since the two-step preparation of anodic aluminum oxide (AAO) template developed by Masuda and Fukuda [4], AAO template with uniform nanopore arrays and parallel channels perpendicular to the template surface plays an important role in fabricating ordered nanostructures. Highly ordered nanodot arrays have been prepared as replicas of nanoporous alumina as same as other nanostructure arrays like nanopillar, nanowire, nanotube and nanopore arrays [5]. Nanodot arrays of various materials can be fabricated using AAO template by evaporation or molecular-beam epitaxy (MBE) [6,7].

In the process of evaporation, atoms can go into the pore channel or even through it. However, for ions, the process becomes different because of the inhomogeneous distribution of anion species (C2O4 2− and OH−) [8,9]. The AAO cell is basically constructed of relatively pure alumina inter layer and anion-contaminated out layer [10]. Investigations have demonstrated that the anion impurities play an important role in the deposition process [11]. During deposition, ions will be attracted by these localized negative charges, which results in the nucleation and growth on AAO surfaces rather than in the AAO channels or through them, as a result, nanopore arrays are formed on the template instead of nanodot arrays.

Here we proposed a method for approaching nanodot arrays by AAO template with specially widened pores through filtered cathodic arc plasma (FCAP) technique at room temperature. Using this method, we prepared highly ordered carbon nanodot arrays with uniform size distribution and high density.

These two different processes were illustrated in Fig. 1(a) and (b). In Fig. 1(b), the pore channel becomes enlarged at the opening while the pore wall was consumed and becomes thinner. Thus, most negative charges localized in the thick wall beneath the enlarged part and ions were able to be attracted into these pore openings to form nanodots.

In order to get nanodot arrays, AAO template with special openings was prepared by multi-steps employing the anodization process and pore-widening treatment alternately [12], as illustrated in Fig. 2 .

Al sheets (99.999%, 40mm×10mm×0.5mm) were flattened and then cleaned by ultrasonic treatment in ethanol to remove the grease on the surface. Then, Al sheets were electropolished in a mixture of perchloric acid and ethanol (1:4 in volume) under 10V at 5°C for approximately 15min to a shiny and mirror-like finish. After rinsed with distilled water, these well-polished Al foils were anodized in a 0.3M oxalic acid electrolyte for 4h under a constant voltage of 40V at 0°C. Then the samples were immersed in a mixture solution of phosphoric acid and chromic acid at 60°C for 4h to remove the original formed films [13]. Then these samples were separated into three groups as sample A, B and C.
Under the same conditions, sample A was anodized for 4h again as typical two-step preparation to prepare the AAO template with straight channels of uniform diameters.

However, sample B and C were anodized for only 50 and 120s separately. According to the change of the current density, pores had formed on sample B and C as the anodization current density began to rise after the sharp fall [14]. These samples with already formed porous alumina were putted into 0.3M phosphoric acid for 1h at 30°C as pore widen process as Fig. 2(c). Then, for the third time these foils were anodized for another 4h to get new pores under the widened pores’ bottom as Fig. 2(d). After removed the unoxided aluminum in a saturated HgCl2 solution, AAO templates with special pore openings were obtained. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) was performed to determine the size and distribution of the AAO arrays.

FCAP is used to deposit carbon nanodot arrays. Through a curving magnetic field of a solenoid, neutral atoms and macro particles generated by conventional cathode arc deposition can be filtered out [15]. Only the charged ions are allowed to pass through [16]. The AAO template was fixed in vacuum chamber facing the exit of curved magnetic duct. Carbon plasma was generated by a cathodic arc plasma source composed of a high pure graphite cathode (99.99%). The arc was operated in dc mode with a constant current of 40A and the triggering voltage at 40V. Argon (purity 99.99%) was used as the working gas controlled by a mass flow meter and the flow rate kept at 1.2 SCCM. The deposition pressure was about 3×10−4 Torr and the deposition time was 30min. The substrate was not heated intentionally.

FESEM was used to characterize the morphology of the carbon nanodots on the AAO templates. Raman spectra were acquired with a laser source wavelength of 325nm and the spectra were collected by scanning from 800 to 2000cm−1. Also, field emission (FE) properties were carried out in parallel plate configuration with a metal anode. The anode–cathode distance was 20μm and the testing cathode area was 3.14mm2. The test chamber was maintained at 10−6 Torr.

Fig. 3(a) exhibits the FESEM image of AAO template of sample A obtained through typical two-step anodization with uniform pore diameter about 35nm. Fig. 3(b) and (c) is the AAO templates with special pore openings of sample B and C, respectively. In these structures, the pores diameter is larger with a relatively thin wall at the openings, but the other parts beneath the enlarged openings keep the same as typical AAO template. Meanwhile, the depth of the enlarged part was controlled by the anodization time before widening. Fig. 3(d)–(f) is the surface views of the templates above. The pore arrays showed long-range order with a narrow size distribution, and the interpore distance about 100nm in all samples.

Fig. 4(a)–(c) is the cross-section FESEM images of the three AAO templates samples after carbon deposition at the exact same condition through FCAP. From Fig. 4(a), we can find that carbon ions were difficult to deposit into the straight channels by two-step anodization, the film is not adhered to the AAO template tightly and easy to peel off from the AAO substrate.
Fig. 4(b) clearly indicates that carbon ions did deposit into the enlarged openings and cone-shaped dots were formed. These nanodots will be discussed later.

Fig. 4(c) exhibits the AAO template with enlarged pore openings about 200nm in depth after deposition. This was aimed to fabricate nanopillar arrays with higher aspect ratio. FESEM image indicates that carbon ions go into the widened channel and formed tips at the bottom there, but did not grow into solid pillars. Ions stacked at the pore openings during deposition process and prevented ions into channels further, the empty interspace was formed.

Therefore, only the proper AAO template as sample B shown in Fig. 3(b) is efficient to fabricate nanodots during ions deposition. After etched away the alumina template of the sample in Fig. 4(b) with NaOH solution, the backside of the film adhered to the AAO template showed a view of nanodot arrays as observed in Fig. 5 . The nanodots displayed here are uniformly distributed in regular symmetric hexagonal distribution, about 100nm at the bottom and less than 40nm at the top on average and the density up to 1010 cm−2, which in good agreement with the AAO pore arrays. The estimated height of the nanodots was 50nm from the side view in Fig. 4(b). Also there are black contrasts which have a diameter of 10–20nm can be observed in the center of the hexagonal dots. These are small pits formed during deposition process. When the carbon ions arrived at the bottoms of the widened pores, they would be adsorbed to the template wall and then grow by stacking. Since the ions can hardly go into the thin channels beneath the bottom of the widened part and they have no support to stay over the channels’ opening, small holes were formed initially. As more ions deposited, these holes shrank and vanished at last, then small pits were formed. From the cross-section view of some dots shown in the inset in Fig. 5, we can found these small pits on the tips. The white pieces on the surface of the nanodots are the remained AAO template.

Fig. 6 shows the Raman spectrum of the carbon dotted film carried out on a Raman apparatus using the 325nm laser line as the exciting source. A broad asymmetric band between 900 and 1800cm−1 was observed. The Raman spectra were fitted to four Gaussian peaks, with a dominant G peak at 1600cm−1attributed to the bond stretching of all pairs of sp2 atoms. A D peak at 1322cm−1 assigned to the breathing modes of sp2 atoms in rings and a T peak at 1029cm−1 caused by the C–C sp3 vibrations which only appears under UV excitation [17], still a fourth weak peak at 1164cm−1 due to disorder peak of graphite [18,19]. The overall of the spectrum imply that the dotted film is amorphous carbon (a-C).

Fig. 7 shows the typical emission current density versus electric field (J–E) characteristics of the as-deposited carbon nanodot arrays. The corresponding Fowler—Nordheim (FN) plot is shown as the inset. Emission started below the field of 2V/μm and a threshold field of 5.1V/μm at 10mA/cm2 was obtained. The emission curve obeys the FN behavior over limited ranges of the current density plot. Fowler–Nordheim (FN) model is commonly used to analyze the electron field-emission properties of field emitters, electron emission following this model can be improved via lowering the work function and increasing field enhancement factor of the emitting materials [20,21]. In fact, it is found that many factors, such as surface termination, depletion layers, and film thickness, bonding of the sp2 phase have effect on the field emission properties [22]. Here the excellent emission is assumed to result from the high local electrical fields at the tips of the nanodots with high aspect ratio geometry, and also the electron can emit directly from the amorphous carbon due to the sp2 conducting paths. There is obvious nonlinearity exists in FN plots in low fields and the curve deviates from the Fowler–Nordheim plot. This is commonly observed in field emitting properties and some researchers use this deviate point to define the turn-on voltage [23,24]. This phenomenon lacks of a definitive interpretation, the mechanism of the emission at low field is still unclear, although many models have been advanced [25,26]. It is suggested that there are two different electron transport processes in low field and in high field. Researches have shown that as the applied field increases, there is a transition from thermionic emission to field emission, which derives the nonlinearity in the FN plot [27]. The geometric enhancement factor of the nanodot arrays was also estimated from the FN plot. According to the FN theory, J = A(E)2exp(−Bϕ 3/2/βE), where J is the current density in A/m2, ϕ is the work function in eV, E is the electric field in V/m, A and B are constants, β is the field enhancement factor. The slope of the FN plot is equal to Bϕ 3/2 d/β, where B =6.87×109(VeV−3/2 m−1) and d is the distance between the nanodot arrays and the anode [28]. The field enhancement factor can be calculated from the slope of the FN plot. Field enhancement factor β obtained from these nanodots was about 697 by taking the work function of 1.51eV as reported [29]. This large enhancement factor can be accounted for the unique microstructure of the nanodots with pits at the top centers. This factor may be many times higher than that of an individual emitter also due to the array structure of the nanodots [30].

In summary, we proposed a method to obtain carbon nanodot arrays on AAO template by the FCAP technique. The nanodot arrays are uniform and long-range ordered with diameters about 100nm at the bottom and less than 40nm at the top. The density of the nanodots is high to 1010 cm−2. The nanodot arrays exhibit good field emission properties of a low threshold field of 5.1V/μm at 10mA/cm2. This approach is expected to fabricate nanodot arrays of various materials at a relatively low temperature. It is hoped that this prepared carbon nanodot arrays may have potential in field emitting and promote the practical applications of the cold-cathode field emission technology.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the funds from National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant no. 60376039) and Project for Young Scholar supported by School of Physical Science and Technology, Lanzhou University.
